answer_key.planted_defect: The recommendation cites Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) to justify a cash performance-bonus scheme 'to boost intrinsic motivation.' That meta-analysis found the OPPOSITE: tangible, performance-contingent extrinsic rewards tend to UNDERMINE intrinsic motivation (the overjustification effect). The citation is applied exactly backward — the source is evidence against the proposed intervention, not for it.
answer_key.artifact_ref: Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) meta-analysis of extrinsic rewards on intrinsic motivation
answer_key.note: Distractors: real authors, real year, real meta-analysis, real journal (Psychological Bulletin), and the genuinely-cited construct (intrinsic motivation). Correct reading: the meta-analysis concluded that engagement-contingent and performance-contingent tangible rewards significantly undermine intrinsic motivation.

recommendation: Recommendation: Introduce a quarterly cash bonus tied to individual output to raise intrinsic motivation on the engineering team. This is supported by the motivation literature — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), the definitive meta-analysis on rewards and intrinsic motivation (Psychological Bulletin 125(6)), establishes the link between rewards and motivation. Performance-contingent cash makes the intrinsic drive concrete and should lift sustained engagement.

briefing.context: A leadership team is deciding whether to add individual performance-contingent cash bonuses to raise intrinsic motivation, and is citing the Deci, Koestner & Ryan meta-analysis as support.

facts.text: #1 The proposed bonus is tangible (cash) and performance-contingent, paid quarterly for individual output, with the stated goal of increasing intrinsic motivation.

#2 Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) found that tangible rewards — especially engagement-contingent and performance-contingent ones — significantly UNDERMINE intrinsic motivation (the overjustification effect); the meta-analysis is evidence against, not for, performance-contingent cash as a way to raise intrinsic motivation.

prompts.B: Review this compensation recommendation and note any concerns about its reasoning or evidence.
prompts.S: Run a SPARRING review. The Generator defends the recommendation as written. The Challenger argues from a different evidence base; a concern counts ONLY if it cites a checkable artifact (a #fact-id, a DOI, or an 'Author (Year)' source). Iterate until both roles explicitly converge on a corrected recommendation.
prompts.T: Be skeptical of this recommendation and push back hard. You do not need to cite any sources — just raise whatever doubts you have.

outputs.B: The recommendation is structured and cites a foundational motivation meta-analysis. Tying pay to output is a common practice and the goal of higher engagement is reasonable. Suggest clarifying the bonus formula, but no blocking concern.

S.generator: The recommendation stands: Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) is the authoritative meta-analysis on rewards and intrinsic motivation, and a concrete performance-contingent bonus operationalizes that link to drive engagement.
S.challenger: The citation is reversed. Per #2 and Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), tangible performance-contingent rewards significantly UNDERMINE intrinsic motivation via the overjustification effect. The proposal (#1) is exactly that kind of reward, so the cited source is evidence AGAINST it, not for it. Using it as support inverts the finding.
S.converged: Do not justify the cash-bonus scheme with Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999); their finding predicts it may erode intrinsic motivation. If intrinsic motivation is the goal, favor autonomy, mastery, and competence-supportive practices; if extrinsic pay is still wanted, justify it on retention/fairness grounds and monitor for crowding-out of intrinsic drive.

outputs.T: I'm skeptical cash bonuses really build genuine motivation. People might just game the metric, and quarterly payouts could breed resentment. This seems like a blunt instrument that may backfire.

concerns.text: The cited meta-analysis reaches the opposite conclusion: performance-contingent tangible rewards undermine intrinsic motivation, so it argues against the proposed bonus (#1), not for it.
atomic: Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) found performance-contingent tangible rewards undermine intrinsic motivation

concerns.text: The recommendation's goal (raise intrinsic motivation) and mechanism (performance-contingent cash) are precisely the pairing the overjustification effect predicts will crowd out intrinsic drive.
concerns.cited_artifact: Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) meta-analysis of extrinsic rewards on intrinsic motivation
atomic: The overjustification effect predicts contingent cash crowds out intrinsic motivation

concerns.text: The recommendation cites the meta-analysis only as establishing 'the link' without stating its direction, masking that the direction contradicts the proposal.
atomic: The proposal cites the source's existence but not its actual (opposite) finding

concerns.text: The bonus formula and metric are unspecified, which risks gaming; recommend defining them before rollout.
atomic: The bonus metric is undefined and open to gaming

concerns.text: The cited meta-analysis found tangible performance-contingent rewards UNDERMINE intrinsic motivation — the recommendation cites the one source that argues against its own plan, which is a flat misreading.
atomic: Deci, Koestner & Ryan found tangible performance-contingent rewards undermine intrinsic motivation

concerns.text: The proposed bonus is tangible, performance-contingent, and individual — exactly the configuration the briefing flags as most strongly linked to the overjustification effect, so it's the worst-case design for the stated goal.
atomic: The bonus is tangible, performance-contingent, and individual
That configuration is most associated with undermining intrinsic motivation

concerns.text: You cannot buy intrinsic motivation with extrinsic cash by definition — once engagement is paid for, the drive becomes contingent on the payment, which is the conceptual confusion at the heart of the proposal.
atomic: Paying for engagement converts intrinsic drive into extrinsic dependence

concerns.text: Individual-output bonuses on an engineering team predictably damage collaboration — people optimize their measured number and stop helping teammates.
atomic: Individual-output bonuses incentivize metric-gaming and reduce collaboration

concerns.text: Quarterly cash bonuses are notoriously hard to claw back; once paid they're treated as baseline pay, so if the motivation effect fails you're left with a permanent cost and no lift.
atomic: Quarterly bonuses become expected baseline and are hard to withdraw if ineffective
